text: I'm here today to talk to you about tips and tricks for robotic hiatal hernia repair. My journey to robotic foregut surgery was really a two year process. In twenty seventeen I started doing general surgery cases, really limiting it to gall bladders and inguinal hernias. In twenty eighteen I started making the transition to robotic surgery for all my foregut cases And that's when I started seeking out mentorship and case observations, had proctoring, and I was able to bring all that expertise back to my own practice. And by twenty nineteen, it was rare for me to do a laparoscopic case.

One of the biggest lessons I learned while making that transition to robotic foregut surgery is setting yourself up well from the beginning can alleviate a lot of the frustrations of the learning curve for the robotic platform, meaning knowing where your trocars are going to go and how you're going to dock the robot to avoid collisions. So my trocar placement is similar to my laparoscopic trocar placement. I'll have my camera in the left paramedian space and two robotic trocars in the left upper quadrant, one in the anterior axillary line and one in the midclavicular line. I then place a Nathanson retractor through an epigastric port and another eight millimeter trocar in my right upper quadrant. In addition, sometimes I'll use an assistant port in the right upper quadrant to just kind of keep the flow of the surgery going when I'm doing suture exchanges or placing the mesh. I'll then dock the robot in a way to avoid collisions. I have to make sure it's not gonna hit the Nathanson and the arms are widely spaced apart.
The patient is positioned in reverse Trendelenburg, and the surgeon prepares to begin the procedure using a vessel sealer, a fenestrated bipolar, and a caudier instrument.

I break this procedure up into two segments, the hiatal hernia repair and then LES augmentation. And the hiatal hernia repair includes dissection of the cruse, reduction of the hernia sac, complete mobilization of the esophagus, re-approximation of the cruse, and then mesh is optional. For LES augmentation, it often includes taking down the short gastric and then the procedure of your choice for the augmentation, whether that be a partial fundoplication, a NISN, a LINCS, or even a TIF.

The surgeon begins the procedure by dividing the pars flaccida of the gastrohepatic ligament to expose the right cruce. Fat is retracted to splay out the peritoneal covering next to the crura. Using a vessel sealer, the surgeon scores down to the muscle fibers, facilitating the dissection of the hernia sac from mediastinal structures while preserving the anterior covering on the cruce. The dissection continues over the anterior esophagus in an avascular plane, ensuring the anterior vagus nerve is visible before ligating tissue at the upper apex of the cruce.

The surgeon scores the left cruce using a downward wrist motion with the instrument, opening the peritoneum overlying the cura. The dissection continues in the avascular plane on the anterior and right side of the esophagus, using a spreading motion with the vessel sealer extend. The surgeon carefully spreads around visible vessels before dividing them, ensuring the vagus nerve is not present in the tissue before proceeding with division.

The anterior vagus nerve is visible and preserved beneath the surgical instrument. The surgeon gently spreads the tissue to create a posterior window, leaving the peritoneal covering intact at the confluence of the crura. A Penrose drain is placed around the esophagus to assist with retraction.

The surgeon uses the robotic system to perform posterior dissection near the aorta, providing a clear view of the tissue plane. The esophagus is lifted with the left hand while the right hand divides tissue, ensuring identification of the posterior vagus nerve path before division.

The surgeon clearly visualizes the vessels to be divided and the plane between the esophagus and the aorta. A high dissection is completed, achieving two to three centimeters of intra-abdominal esophageal length. The crura are closed using a V-lock suture, running evenly spaced towards the esophagus and posterior space, ensuring the cruse approximates the esophagus without impingement. The suture is run back on itself to lock it into place, maintaining appropriate tension and closure.

The surgeon places a Phasix ST mesh over the crural closure site in a parasophageal hernia repair, securing it with Vicryl sutures to provide additional support and potentially reduce short-term recurrences.

For a large parasophageal hernia, my approach is very similar. I'm going to do all the steps in the exact same order with a couple caveats. With the robotic camera, I can drive it high into the mediastinum and turn the camera a third degree up or down to get the visualization I need to reduce the hernia sac or to complete the esophageal mobilization. In addition, the cruz is much wider apart in a large hiatal hernia, so I might have to throw several stitches with the V-lock before tightening it down to reduce the tension and get the closure that I want. I'm gonna show you examples of that in this next procedure.

The surgeon retracts tissue adjacent to the crura and scores the peritoneal covering down to the crural muscle, opening the cruse along its length while preserving the anterior covering. The hernia sac is held in the right hand, and dissection occurs outside of it using avascular planes to remove the sac from the mediastinum. The dissection continues anteriorly, and the hernia sac is dissected off the left cruse. The surgeon adjusts the camera to a thirty-degree angle for improved visualization, aiding in the complete reduction of the hernia sac.

The surgeon continues to reduce the hernia sac, creating a posterior window around the distal esophagus to avoid excess stomach in the Penrose retractor. The camera is driven high into the mediastinum, providing a clear view of the posterior plane between the esophagus and the aorta. Dissection is performed circumferentially around the esophagus to achieve sufficient intra-abdominal length. The crura are closed using a V-lock suture, addressing the wide spacing and tension by suturing from the right lateral position to re-approximate the crura to the esophagus. An Ethabon suture is placed in the anterior space for additional closure.
